questions.question: A Django voting view runs: selected = question.choices.get(pk=choice_id); selected.votes += 1; selected.save(update_fields=['votes']). A load test fires 1,000 concurrent POSTs at one choice, but the final votes value lands well under 1,000. What is the defect, and the idiomatic fix?
choices: The save must run inside transaction.atomic: without an explicit transaction each save() auto-commits independently, and concurrent auto-commits overwrite each other. Wrapping the read and the save in one atomic block makes the increment safe
QuerySets are lazy, so votes may come from a cached earlier evaluation; calling refresh_from_db() right before saving guarantees each increment starts from the current committed value

The code is a read-modify-write: every request copies votes into Python, adds 1, and writes back its private sum — two concurrent voters both read N and both write N+1, losing an increment. F('votes') + 1 pushes the arithmetic into the UPDATE statement itself (SET votes = votes + 1), so the database applies every increment atomically no matter how many writers race

update_fields=['votes'] is the bug: narrowing the write bypasses the change-detection save() normally performs, so concurrent saves can no longer see each other's writes; dropping the argument restores the full-row write and with it the lost updates
questions.explanation: This is the classic lost-update race, and the fix is architectural: move the computation to where the data lives. As long as Python does the addition, some interleaving of read-add-write will overwrite a concurrent increment — no transaction wrapper changes that, because atomic() guarantees all-or-nothing commit, not serialized isolation; two atomic blocks can still both read N (you would need select_for_update's row lock to serialize them, a much heavier tool). F() sidesteps locking entirely by making the database do the math inside a single UPDATE, which is atomic by definition. The other distractors misread the mechanics: refresh_from_db() merely shrinks the race window — a stale read-then-write is still possible in the gap; and update_fields only narrows which columns get written (a good habit that avoids clobbering other fields and firing stale auto_now values), it neither adds nor removes concurrency detection, because save() has none.

questions.question: During an incident, every target in an ALB target group is failing its health checks — yet the ALB keeps routing requests to them and a fraction succeed, so the traffic dashboard looks alive. The on-call engineer concludes the health checks must be misconfigured. What is actually happening, and what is the monitoring lesson?
choices: Targets are only marked unhealthy after the deregistration delay (default 300 s) has drained their in-flight connections; during that window they still receive new requests, which explains the traffic. Shortening deregistration_delay makes health checks take effect immediately

The ALB is failing open: when every target in a group is unhealthy, it routes to all of them anyway, on the logic that a struggling target beats no target at all. The lesson is that traffic flowing is not proof of health — a green request graph can hide a fully unhealthy fleet. Alarm on UnHealthyHostCount and on HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count (load-balancer errors, distinct from your app's 5XX), not on whether requests succeed

Cross-zone load balancing is masking the failure: the ALB silently shifts traffic to healthy targets in other Availability Zones, and the dashboard aggregates across zones. Disabling cross-zone balancing would have surfaced the outage per zone

Listener rules forward directly to a target group without consulting health status — only round-robin selection inside a healthy group does. With a default rule in place, unhealthy targets remain reachable; the fix is a fixed-response rule guarding the group
questions.explanation: Fail-open is a deliberate design trade: if the ALB honored health checks when zero targets pass, it would convert 'every target is struggling' into 'every request is guaranteed to fail'. Routing to all of them instead preserves whatever capacity remains — perhaps the health endpoint itself broke while the app partly works. The engineering lesson is about observability: health state and traffic success are separate signals, and only the metrics expose the first — UnHealthyHostCount says probes are failing, and HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count separates 'the load balancer had no healthy target' from 'the application returned 500'. The distractors bend real mechanisms: deregistration delay governs graceful connection draining when you remove a target, not how quickly failing health checks bite; cross-zone balancing spreads load across zones, but the premise is that every target is unhealthy — there is nowhere to shift to; and every forward action, from any rule, selects among the target group's healthy members — rules choose the group, health filtering happens inside it.

questions.question: A story carries this AC: 'The backend caches search results in Redis with a 60-second TTL.' The author defends it as a good criterion because it is objectively verifiable — you can inspect Redis and measure the TTL. What is wrong with it?
choices: The content is fine but the format is wrong: caching behavior involves state and triggers, so it must be expressed as a Given/When/Then scenario rather than a declarative sentence
It is a Definition of Done item mislabeled as an AC: infrastructure concerns like caching belong to the team-wide quality gates that apply to every story, not to story-specific criteria

The hard-coded 60 seconds is the flaw: durations are tuning decisions that change after profiling, so AC should express intent ('results are reasonably fresh') and leave the numbers to the implementation

It specifies how, not what. AC describe observable system behavior — what a tester, PO, or customer outside the team would call 'the thing working' — and Redis is a design choice, not a behavior. Verifiable-but-internal is still an anti-pattern: it freezes one implementation, failing acceptance if the mechanism changes while behavior stays intact. The rewrite states the observable contract, e.g. 'repeated identical searches within 60 seconds return consistent results with perceived latency under 50 ms' — which a Redis cache is then one way to satisfy
questions.explanation: Objective verifiability is necessary but not sufficient — the criterion must also sit at the right layer: observable behavior, never mechanism. An AC that names Redis couples acceptance to a design decision: swap Redis for an in-process cache and the story 'fails' while users see identical behavior; keep Redis but misconfigure it and the story 'passes' while users see stale results. Writing the contract behaviorally keeps the design space open and makes the criterion meaningful to someone outside the team. The distractors each miss that layer: format choice is orthogonal — a declarative checklist is legitimately right-sized for threshold assertions like the rewrite; the Definition of Done holds team-wide gates ('code reviewed', 'deployed to staging') that apply to every story, which a story-specific caching behavior is not; and concrete numbers are exactly what good AC use — vague terms like 'reasonably fresh' are the anti-pattern (SMART's M), so the problem is where the detail points, not that it is precise.

questions.question: A team migrates from XGBoost, where max_depth=6 was their main overfitting control, to LightGBM. They carry max_depth=6 over, leave num_leaves at its default 31, and still overfit. The tuning guidance says capacity in LightGBM is governed by num_leaves and min_data_in_leaf, with max_depth demoted to a 'safety rail'. What about leaf-wise growth changes which dial matters?
choices: Level-wise trees expand the whole frontier floor by floor, so depth ≈ capacity and a depth cap is a real capacity dial. Leaf-wise growth instead always splits the single best-gain leaf in the whole tree, producing asymmetric trees whose depth is emergent — capacity is the leaf count, and the characteristic overfit is deep, thin branches chasing tiny pockets of residual noise. num_leaves caps total capacity and min_data_in_leaf makes those noise pockets unreachable; a depth cap constrains the wrong dimension of an asymmetric tree

num_leaves and max_depth are mutually exclusive in LightGBM: setting both causes the library to ignore both and fall back to unconstrained growth, which is why carrying max_depth over silently removed all capacity control

Leaf-wise trees are systematically shallower than level-wise trees of equal accuracy, so a depth-6 cap never binds; the overfitting comes from histogram binning noise, which min_data_in_leaf exists to smooth out

Histograms quantize every feature into at most 255 bins, blurring split thresholds so that depth no longer correlates with model complexity; min_data_in_leaf compensates by forcing more rows through each blurred split
questions.explanation: The right dial follows the growth strategy. Level-wise growth finishes every node at one depth before descending, so a depth-d tree has a predictable symmetric shape — capping depth caps capacity. Leaf-wise growth spends its split budget wherever gain is highest, growing deep exactly where residuals still pay; its failure mode is a few far-down leaves memorizing noise, which is why the brake is a per-leaf row floor (min_data_in_leaf) plus a total leaf cap (num_leaves), not a depth ceiling the asymmetric shape mostly ignores. There is also a silent interaction worth knowing: when both are set, the smaller of num_leaves and 2^max_depth rules with no warning — here 31 < 2^6 = 64, so the carried-over depth cap was not even the binding constraint. The distractors invent mechanics: setting both dials never disables them (the tighter one wins silently); leaf-wise trees grow deeper, not shallower, where it pays; and histogram binning is a split-finding speed trick whose accuracy cost is famously near zero — it is unrelated to capacity control.

questions.question: In backlog review you meet: 'As a QA engineer, I can have the test database restored automatically each night, so that I can start testing on clean data without waiting for ops.' It is perfectly Connextra-shaped — concrete role, clear goal, stated benefit. The zones-of-control frame still flags it. What is the diagnosis?
choices: Dependency-Locked: nightly database restores require infrastructure owned by the ops team, putting the deliverable outside the delivery team's Zone of Control and making the story unschedulable in a sprint

Misleading Story: 'restored automatically each night' prescribes a mechanism, hiding the real problem; rewriting the goal as 'I can always test against clean data' would restore negotiability and fix the story

Fake Story: the 'so that' need sits inside the team's own Zone of Control rather than in the Sphere of Influence where user behaviour and business outcomes live. When the team can simply do the thing the benefit names, the story is 'effectively a task without risk' — the team is writing itself tickets. The honest fix is to track it as a Task, not to dress internal work in story costume
It fails INVEST-Valuable: a QA engineer is an internal role, not a paying customer, so no story may name one; rewriting it around an end-user role who benefits from faster releases would repair it
questions.explanation: The zones frame reads a story by where its two halves live: a healthy story's deliverable ('I can…') sits in the Zone of Control — things the team changes on its own — while its need ('so that…') sits in the Sphere of Influence — user behaviour and business outcomes the team can affect but not decree. Here both halves are inside the Zone of Control: the team can build the restore job, and the beneficiary is the team itself, so no external risk is being managed — the defining trait of the Fake Story. The point is planning honesty, not format policing: internal enablement work is often worth doing, so track it as a Task under its real justification. The distractors misapply neighboring concepts: Dependency-Locked is the opposite geometry (the deliverable sits outside the Zone of Control, like an unsigned vendor integration); a Misleading Story requires a hidden user problem behind the prescribed mechanism, and rephrasing this one would only produce a smoother Fake Story since no user need exists; and INVEST-V measures value to the named role — inventing an end-user role to launder an internal task adds fiction, not value.

questions.question: You point boto3 at the local MinIO container (endpoint_url='http://minio:9000'), and every S3 call fails with a connection error mentioning a host named user-uploads.minio. The identical code works against real AWS. What design difference explains the mangled hostname, and what is the fix?
choices: boto3 defaults to virtual-hosted-style addressing, which encodes the bucket in the hostname (bucket.s3.region.amazonaws.com) — a scheme that works on AWS only because AWS operates DNS for every bucket subdomain. Against a custom endpoint, user-uploads.minio resolves to nothing. Path-style addressing keeps the host fixed and carries the bucket in the URL path (host/bucket/key), which works against any endpoint: set addressing_style 'path' in the client's Config

MinIO requires buckets to exist before any call, and the odd hostname is boto3's fallback lookup for a missing bucket; running the mc mb setup step so user-uploads exists makes the client revert to normal addressing

The endpoint_url must include the bucket (http://minio:9000/user-uploads) because MinIO, unlike AWS, cannot infer the bucket from credentials; without it boto3 guesses by prepending the bucket to the host
Docker's embedded DNS refuses dotted service names; adding a network alias 'user-uploads.minio' to the MinIO service resolves the hostname and fixes the calls without touching client configuration
questions.explanation: S3 has two URL shapes, and the failure is the gap between them. Virtual-hosted style turns every bucket into a DNS name — viable when Amazon runs the DNS for *.s3.region.amazonaws.com, impossible for an arbitrary endpoint like a compose-stack container. Path-style exists for exactly this case: one fixed host, bucket in the path, so the same client works against any S3-compatible server. That is why the missing addressing_style is called the single most common MinIO-from-SDK mistake — the storage code is correct, the addressing contract is not. The distractors confuse layers: a genuinely missing bucket produces an S3-level NoSuchBucket 404 after a successful connection, never a DNS failure, and bucket existence has no effect on URL formation; endpoint_url is a base host by contract — the SDK composes the bucket into host or path according to the addressing style, and stuffing the bucket into the endpoint fights that machinery; and a Docker network alias can paper over one bucket's hostname, but it hand-rebuilds AWS's per-bucket DNS for every bucket you ever create — re-implementing the very requirement path-style removes.

questions.question: Orders must be soft-deleted — archived, never removed. A developer overrides the viewset's destroy() to call order_service.archive(kwargs['pk']) and return 204. It works in manual testing, but review rejects it: DELETE with an unknown id also returns 204, and the override rebuilt plumbing DRF already provides. What is the sanctioned pattern?
choices: Return 200 with the archived row's serialized body instead of 204, so clients can distinguish a soft delete from a hard delete; DRF reserves 204 No Content for physical row removal

Register a separate @action(detail=True, methods=['post'], url_path='archive') endpoint and leave destroy untouched — routers reserve the destroy action for genuine row deletion, and overloading DELETE breaks the router's URL contract

Override get_queryset() to return Order.objects.filter(archived=False): DRF then treats archived rows as absent everywhere, and destroy can stay stock because hiding a row is equivalent to deleting it at the API surface

Use the handler/hook split every DRF write action is built on: destroy(request) is the HTTP layer — it resolves the row via self.get_object() (the fetch-or-404 contract) and shapes the 204 response — and delegates the actual work to perform_destroy(instance), a one-line hook. Swapping what deletion means is exactly the hook's job: override perform_destroy to set archived=True and save. The rejected code broke the contract by skipping get_object(), so unknown ids flowed into the service and silently no-opped into a 204 instead of a 404
questions.explanation: DRF splits every write action into an HTTP handler (destroy, create, update) and a work hook (perform_destroy, perform_create, perform_update) precisely so policy changes slot in without re-implementing wire mechanics. Overriding the hook keeps DRF's URL resolution, object-level permission checks, and status-code contract intact while redefining what the operation means — soft delete is the textbook case. Overriding the handler is reserved for when the whole act is delegated (say, a service owning a multi-row transaction), and even then the rule is to call self.get_object() first, because that call is where 404-for-unknown-id and permission enforcement live. The distractors each solve a different problem: 204 No Content is destroy's response contract regardless of storage strategy — leaking hard-vs-soft through status codes couples clients to persistence details; a custom archive action is a legitimate API-design debate but sidesteps the review finding, leaving DELETE broken; and filtering the queryset governs what reads can see — a useful complement that performs no archiving at all.

questions.question: A BDD suite has 45 Gherkin scenarios, all executed through the browser: 5 happy paths and 40 validation, permission, and limit sad paths. CI takes 50 minutes and flakes weekly. A teammate proposes deleting the sad-path scenarios and rewriting them as plain unit tests. What does the guidance actually prescribe?
choices: Keep all 45 in the browser and parallelize CI instead: acceptance scenarios are end-to-end by definition, and moving any of them off the UI layer downgrades them from acceptance tests to integration tests

Push scenarios down the pyramid without abandoning the discipline: acceptance scenarios are not chained to the top layer. Keep the happy path in the browser — one scenario per slice, proving the whole thing hangs together — and run the sad paths as the same Gherkin, at the API layer: no browser, the same specification-and-verification role, a fraction of the cost and flake
Accept the proposal: Gherkin's ceremony is only justified for the happy paths stakeholders actually read, and validation rules are implementation detail best covered by developer-facing unit tests

Collapse the 40 sad paths into a few Scenario Outlines: one scenario template with a large Examples table per rule family cuts scenario setup overhead, keeping full browser coverage at a fraction of the runtime
questions.explanation: The pyramid rule and the BDD discipline are orthogonal, and the guidance keeps both: 'there's technically no need to write acceptance tests at the highest level — push your tests as far down the test pyramid as you can.' Gherkin's value is that the scenarios are the specification — business-readable, and drift fails the build — and that property does not depend on which layer executes the steps. Running sad paths against the API keeps every rule inside the living documentation while removing the browser's cost and flakiness; the single UI happy path still proves the slice integrates end to end. The distractors each sacrifice one half: 'acceptance means E2E' is exactly the misconception the guidance rejects; rewriting rules as unit tests silently deletes them from the living documentation, so the specification can drift from the system again without failing anything; and Scenario Outline through the UI is a named anti-pattern — each Examples row still drives the full browser flow, so the table multiplies runtime instead of cutting it.

questions.question: Migrating from a single EC2 host (NGINX at the edge) to ECS behind an ALB, a team copies their VPN-only block for /admin — allow 10.8.0.0/24; deny all; — into the per-task NGINX config. After cutover, every /admin request is denied, including from the VPN. Why, and where must the rule live now?
choices: The task's security group now admits traffic only from the ALB's security group, which rewrites the packet source to the ALB's address; adding the VPN CIDR to the task security group restores the original source IP

The ALB terminates TLS, so the per-task NGINX receives plain HTTP — and location-level allow/deny directives are only evaluated on TLS-terminated server blocks; re-terminating TLS at the task's NGINX re-enables the rules

In the old topology NGINX was the edge, so each connection's source address was the real client and the allowlist worked on it. Behind an ALB, the per-task NGINX sees the load balancer's IP on every connection: 10.8.0.0/24 never matches, so deny all catches everyone — VPN included. IP filtering must move to the layer that still sees the client: an ALB listener rule with a source_ip condition (the ALB now plays the L7-edge role), or NGINX trusting X-Forwarded-For from the ALB

Cross-zone load balancing rotates requests across ALB nodes whose addresses change over time, so any IP-based allowlist behind an ALB is inherently unstable; the fix is fronting the service with a Network Load Balancer, whose static IPs can be allowlisted
questions.explanation: The rule didn't break — its input did. NGINX allow/deny evaluates the TCP connection's source address, and moving NGINX from the edge to behind a proxy changes what that address is: the ALB opens its own connections to the task, so every request arrives 'from' the ALB. The general principle: L7 access control must live wherever the real client identity is visible — at the new edge (ALB listener rules with source_ip conditions), or downstream only via an explicitly trusted header chain (X-Forwarded-For from a proxy you control). That is exactly why, on the ECS path, VPN restrictions are ALB rules rather than task-level nginx deny blocks. The distractors attribute the failure to the wrong layers: security groups admit or drop packets but never rewrite source addresses — and if the SG were blocking, requests would time out rather than get denied responses; TLS termination changes what NGINX decrypts, not whether location rules run — allow/deny works fine on plain HTTP; and rotating node IPs are why clients use the ALB's DNS name, irrelevant to how NGINX evaluates an inbound connection's source.

questions.question: CAS leaves almost everything to judgment, yet is strict about one mechanism: every dictionary-sense use of a defined term must be backticked in Stories and AC (Rule 1). A reviewer calls the backticks decoration and proposes dropping them to cut authoring friction. What makes the highlighting load-bearing rather than cosmetic?
choices: Backticks render as monospace in every markdown viewer, so defined vocabulary is instantly distinguishable while scanning — the framework trades authoring friction for reading speed on the far more frequent read path

The highlight is one half of a propagation contract. When a definition changes, Rule 9 says: grep for the backticked term, and the artifacts needing re-review are exactly those that match — the change's blast radius is computed mechanically instead of recalled from memory. Without Rule 9 the backticks would indeed be decoration; with it, the Dictionary becomes a navigable index of the spec. Dropping the highlighting doesn't just lose styling — it silently breaks change propagation, the framework's defense against definitions drifting out from under the artifacts that cite them
The .feature exporter keys on the backticks: it strips them when deriving runner-executable Gherkin, so unhighlighted terms survive into the export and break step-definition matching downstream

The recurring pruning review counts each term's citations by matching backticked occurrences; without highlighting, zero-citation terms become undetectable and the Dictionary bloats past the lean bar the framework depends on
questions.explanation: The test for 'load-bearing' is: what breaks when you remove it? Rule 1 exists for Rule 9. A shared vocabulary is only trustworthy if definition changes propagate to every artifact written under the old meaning, and CAS makes that propagation mechanical: the backtick marks 'this word is used in its dictionary sense', so a grep returns the exact re-review set — no false positives from casual uses of the same word, no forgotten Stories. That contract is what justifies paying the highlighting cost on every Story and AC; backticks specifically were chosen because they render as monospace everywhere, survive format conversion as literal characters, and are mechanically greppable. The distractors name real but non-load-bearing properties: reading speed is a side benefit, not the justification; the exporter does strip backticks, but the runner matches on the stripped text — a missing backtick yields an inconsistent export, not a step-matching failure; and citation counting for pruning is explicitly judgment-based today ('grep by hand or ask'), with an automatic citation index only a documented future enhancement.
